---
# BLIND-REVIEW SWAPS (currently in CAMERA-READY mode).
# To switch to ARR blind mode:
#   • set _quarto.yml to `anonymous: true, review: true`
#   • replace the title below with the bare "Benchmarking …" form
#   • comment out the `author:` and `author-tex:` blocks
#   • apply the per-paragraph BLIND-REVIEW SWAP comments throughout the body
#   • comment out the Future Work section (body content; blind cap is 8 pages)
#   • comment out the Acknowledgments section
#   • comment out Appendix N (Responsible NLP Checklist)
#
# Blind title:
#   title: "Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law"
title: "BenGER: Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law"
jupyter: benger-dataset-arr
author:
  - name: Sebastian Nagl
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    email: sebastian.nagl@tum.de
    attributes:
      corresponding: true
    note: "Core contributor"
  - name: Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer
    affiliation: Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Martin Heidebach
    affiliation: Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Aleyna Koçak
    affiliation: University of Konstanz
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Anne Zettelmeier
    affiliation: University of Saarbrücken
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Elly Breu
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Angelina Greiner
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Sofija Milijas
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Dataset contributor"
  - name: Matthias Grabmair
    affiliation: Technical University of Munich (TUM)
    note: "Senior author"
author-tex: |
  \author{
    Sebastian Nagl\textsuperscript{1}\thanks{\,Core Contributor, corresponding author. Correspondence to \texttt{sebastian.nagl@tum.de}.} \quad
    Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer\textsuperscript{2\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Martin Heidebach\textsuperscript{2\textdaggerdbl} \\
    \bfseries Aleyna Koçak\textsuperscript{3\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Anne Zettelmeier\textsuperscript{4\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Elly Breu\textsuperscript{1\textdaggerdbl} \\
    \bfseries Angelina Greiner\textsuperscript{1\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Sofija Milijas\textsuperscript{1\textdaggerdbl} \quad
    Matthias Grabmair\textsuperscript{1}\thanks{\,Senior author.} \\[6pt]
    \mdseries \textsuperscript{1}Technical University of Munich (TUM) \quad
    \textsuperscript{2}Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich (LMU) \\
    \textsuperscript{3}University of Konstanz \quad
    \textsuperscript{4}University of Saarbrücken\thanks{\,Dataset contributor.}
  }
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

import json
from pathlib import Path
from collections import Counter

def load_json(path):
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return json.load(f)

def load_optional(path):
    try:
        return load_json(path)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return None

interim   = Path("data/interim")
raw       = Path("data/raw")
processed = Path("data/processed")

zjs = load_json(interim / "zjs.json")
grundprinzipien = load_json(interim / "grundprinzipien.json")
benchathon = load_json(interim / "benchathon.json")
variants = load_json(processed / "benchathon_instruction_variants.json")
benchathon_tasks = load_json(raw / "benchathon" / "Benchathon-tasks-2026-05-23.json")["tasks"]

# Derived exports — produced by scripts/derive_paper_exports.py from the real
# Benchathon export + scripts/derive_inter_judge_agreement.py.
# Loaded if present so the manuscript still renders before derive has run.
systems_meta        = load_optional(processed / "systems.json")
model_evals         = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_model_evaluations.json")
human_grades        = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_human_grades.json")
automatic_metrics   = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_automatic_metrics.json")
human_judge_repeats = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_human_judge_repeats.json")
zjs_summary         = load_optional(processed / "zjs_model_summary.json")

n_zjs = len(zjs)
n_qa = len(grundprinzipien)
n_benchathon = len(benchathon)

_graded_ann_ids = {
    ev["annotation_id"]
    for t in benchathon_tasks
    for ev in t.get("evaluations", [])
    if "human:loesung" in (ev.get("field_name") or "")
    and ev.get("annotation_id") is not None
}
variant_counts = Counter(variants.get(aid) for aid in _graded_ann_ids)
n_human_only = variant_counts["no_ai"]
n_co_creation = variant_counts["ai"]
n_human_total = n_human_only + n_co_creation

# ---- Inline-expression bindings for prose: ----
agreement_stats_setup = load_optional(processed / "agreement_stats.json") or {}
r3 = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_metric_correlation", {})
r3_humans = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_metric_correlation_humans") or {}
CANONICAL_METRIC_ORDER = (
    "bleu", "rouge", "meteor", "chrf",
    "bertscore", "moverscore", "semantic_similarity",
)
METRIC_LABELS = {
    "bleu": "BLEU", "rouge": "ROUGE", "meteor": "METEOR", "chrf": "chrF",
    "bertscore": "BERTScore", "moverscore": "MoverScore",
    "semantic_similarity": "Semantic sim.",
}
r4 = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation", {})
r4_bereich = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_by_bereich", {}) or {}
r5_jh = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_vs_human", {})
r5_irr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_human_irr", {})
r5_cal = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_calderon", {})
r5_dim = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_dim", {})
r5_jr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_repeats", {}) or {}
r4h = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq4_cocreation_blind_human") or {}
r5_creator = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_creator_vs_blind") or {}
r5_llm = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq5_judge_on_llm_solutions") or {}
inter_judge = load_optional(processed / "benchathon_inter_judge_agreement.json") or {}
tier_agg = agreement_stats_setup.get("tier_aggregates") or {}
rank_corr = agreement_stats_setup.get("rq3_rank_correlation") or {}
zjs_metric_corr = load_optional(processed / "zjs_metric_correlation.json") or {}
grundprinzipien_summary  = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_model_summary.json") or []
grundprinzipien_metric_corr = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_metric_correlation.json") or {}
grundprinzipien_tier_agg = load_optional(processed / "grundprinzipien_tier_aggregates.json") or {}

def _zmc(metric, field="pearson_vs_judge_raw"):
    """Safe deep-get for ZJS per-generation metric correlation."""
    return (zjs_metric_corr.get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def _gmc(metric, field="pearson_vs_judge_raw"):
    """Safe deep-get for Grundprinzipien per-generation metric correlation."""
    return (grundprinzipien_metric_corr.get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def _ta(corpus, dim, key, field):
    """Safe deep-get for tier_aggregates so the prose tolerates missing keys.
    `corpus` is benchathon/zjs (sourced from agreement_stats.json) or
    grundprinzipien (sourced from the standalone tier file)."""
    if corpus == "grundprinzipien":
        return ((grundprinzipien_tier_agg.get(dim) or {}).get(key) or {}).get(field)
    return (((tier_agg.get(corpus) or {}).get(dim) or {}).get(key) or {}).get(field)

def _rc(corpus, metric, field="spearman_metric_vs_judge"):
    """Safe deep-get for rq3_rank_correlation."""
    return ((rank_corr.get(corpus) or {}).get(metric) or {}).get(field)

def fmt(x, w=2):
    return "—" if x is None else f"{x:.{w}f}"
def fmtp(x, w=2):
    return "—" if x is None else f"{x:+.{w}f}"
def fmtpct(x, w=0):
    return "—" if x is None else f"{x*100:.{w}f}%"

# Precomputed sentences that render only when their underlying block populates.
# Each is an empty string until the real human-grading layer makes the block
# return non-null. Referenced as `{python} rq4_human_sentence` etc. in prose.
rq4_human_sentence = (
    f" Under the same comparison on the mean of the blind human reviewers, "
    f"the trad-vs-AI difference is {fmtp(r4h.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'), 1)} raw points "
    f"(mean traditional {fmt(r4h.get('mean_traditional'), 1)}; "
    f"mean co-creation {fmt(r4h.get('mean_co_creation'), 1)}; "
    f"bootstrap 95% CI [{fmtp(r4h.get('ci95_lo'), 1)}, {fmtp(r4h.get('ci95_hi'), 1)}])."
    if r4h else ""
)
rq5_creator_sentence = (
    f" The author-informed creator reviews, compared against the mean of the three "
    f"blind reviewers, show a Pearson r={fmt(r5_creator.get('pearson_creator_vs_mean_blind_raw'))} "
    f"(mean creator − mean blind = {fmtp(r5_creator.get('mean_creator_minus_blind_raw'), 1)} "
    f"raw points), confirming that the creator layer captures the same per-solution "
    f"ordering as the blind pool while sitting on a slightly shifted absolute scale."
    if r5_creator else ""
)
rq5_llm_sentence = (
    f" On the {r5_llm.get('n_generations') if r5_llm else 0} LLM-generated solutions "
    f"in the validation subset, the judge agrees with the mean of the blind human "
    f"reviewers at Pearson r={fmt(r5_llm.get('pearson_raw')) if r5_llm else '—'} "
    f"(MAE {fmt(r5_llm.get('mae_raw'), 1) if r5_llm else '—'} raw points), in line "
    f"with the judge–human agreement on human-authored solutions reported above."
    if r5_llm else ""
)
# Single string the prose inlines after the dimension MAE sentence.
rq5_extension = rq5_creator_sentence + rq5_llm_sentence

# Pre-extracted dimension values (the inline-expression form
# `r5_dim.get('x', {}).get('y')` has historically tripped Quarto's
# markdown lexer because of the empty-dict literal `{}`).
def _dim(dim_name, key):
    return ((r5_dim or {}).get(dim_name) or {}).get(key)

dim_pearson_ergebnis      = _dim("ergebnisrichtigkeit", "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_vollstaendig  = _dim("vollstaendigkeit",    "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_rechtsgr      = _dim("rechtsgrundlagen",    "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_rechtsk       = _dim("rechtskenntnis",      "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_subsum        = _dim("subsumtion",          "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_schwerpunkt   = _dim("schwerpunktsetzung",  "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_method        = _dim("methodischer_stil",   "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_gliederung    = _dim("gliederung",          "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_sprache       = _dim("sprache",             "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_pearson_formalia      = _dim("formalia",            "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human")
dim_mae_ergebnis          = _dim("ergebnisrichtigkeit", "mae_dim_points")
dim_mae_formalia          = _dim("formalia",            "mae_dim_points")

# Friendly labels for the rubric dimensions, in the same casing as the prose.
_dim_label = {
    "ergebnisrichtigkeit": "Result correctness",
    "vollstaendigkeit":    "Issue identification",
    "rechtsgrundlagen":    "Legal grounding",
    "rechtskenntnis":      "Doctrinal knowledge",
    "subsumtion":          "Subsumption quality",
    "schwerpunktsetzung":  "Problem depth",
    "methodischer_stil":   "Methodological structure",
    "gliederung":          "Organization",
    "sprache":             "Terminology",
    "formalia":            "Formal correctness",
}

# Sorted dim Pearson list — drives the "highest on / lowest on" prose in RQ5.
_dim_pearson_sorted = sorted(
    (
        (label, _dim(dim, "pearson_judge_vs_mean_human"))
        for dim, label in _dim_label.items()
    ),
    key=lambda kv: (kv[1] if kv[1] is not None else float("-inf")),
    reverse=True,
)
def _dim_prose_top(n):
    items = [(lbl, v) for lbl, v in _dim_pearson_sorted[:n] if v is not None]
    return ", ".join(f"{lbl} (r={fmt(v)})" for lbl, v in items)
def _dim_prose_bottom(n):
    items = [(lbl, v) for lbl, v in _dim_pearson_sorted[-n:] if v is not None]
    return ", ".join(f"{lbl} (r={fmt(v)})" for lbl, v in items)

# Short alias used throughout the paper for each model id; full ids stay
# in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex} (Appendix C) so the body prose can reference
# systems compactly. Keep this dict in sync with the systems_meta names.
MODEL_ALIAS = {
    "claude-sonnet-4-6":                              "Sonnet-4.6",
    "claude-opus-4-7":                                "Opus-4.7",
    "gpt-5.4":                                        "GPT-5.4",
    "gpt-5.4-mini":                                   "GPT-5.4-mini",
    "gpt-5-mini":                                     "GPT-5-mini",
    "gemini-3.1-pro-preview":                         "Gemini-3.1-Pro",
    "gemini-3-flash-preview":                         "Gemini-3-Flash",
    "gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview":                  "Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite",
    "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro":                    "DeepSeek-V4-Pro",
    "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash":                  "DeepSeek-V4-Flash",
    "Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507":             "Qwen3-235B",
    "Qwen/Qwen3.5-122B-A10B":                         "Qwen3.5-122B",
    "Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B":                           "Qwen3.6-35B",
    "meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8": "Llama-4",
    # systems.json strips provider prefixes from open-weight names; alias both forms.
    "Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507":                  "Qwen3-235B",
    "Qwen3.5-122B-A10B":                              "Qwen3.5-122B",
    "Qwen3.6-35B-A3B":                                "Qwen3.6-35B",
    "Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8":         "Llama-4",
}

def alias_for(model_id):
    """Return the short alias for a model id, falling back to the id itself."""
    return MODEL_ALIAS.get(model_id, model_id)

# Per-system mean judge raw on Benchathon (for RQ1 prose).
from statistics import mean as _mean
bench_by_sys = {}
if model_evals:
    _acc = {}
    for r in model_evals:
        if r.get("raw_score") is not None:
            _acc.setdefault(r["system"], []).append(r["raw_score"])
    bench_by_sys = {k: _mean(v) for k, v in _acc.items()}

zjs_by_sys = {}
if zjs_summary:
    for r in zjs_summary:
        zjs_by_sys[r["system"]] = r["metrics"]

grundprinzipien_by_sys = {}
for r in grundprinzipien_summary:
    grundprinzipien_by_sys[r["system"]] = r["metrics"]

def b(sys_id):
    """Mean Benchathon judge raw for a system id, or None."""
    return bench_by_sys.get(sys_id)
def z(sys_id, key="llm_judge_falloesung_raw_mean"):
    return (zjs_by_sys.get(sys_id) or {}).get(key)
def g(sys_id, key="judge_raw_mean"):
    """Per-system Grundprinzipien metric (judge_raw_mean / judge_pass_rate /
    accuracy / <metric>_mean), 0-100 for judge raw, 0-1 for accuracy."""
    return (grundprinzipien_by_sys.get(sys_id) or {}).get(key)

def placeholder_panel(figsize, title, missing_files, hint):
    import matplotlib.pyplot as _plt
    fig, ax = _plt.subplots(figsize=figsize)
    ax.axis("off")
    body = f"{title}\n\nPending export(s):\n  • " + "\n  • ".join(missing_files) + f"\n\n{hint}"
    ax.text(0.02, 0.98, body, ha="left", va="top", fontsize=7,
            family="monospace", color="#444",
            bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.5", fc="#fff8dc", ec="#d4a017"))
    _plt.tight_layout(pad=0.3)
    return fig, ax

# Per-condition human-judge scores on Benchathon (raws, passed). Used by the
# RQ4 fig-human-vs-ai chunk (body) and the main leaderboard chunk; hoisted
# into setup so the figure can run before the leaderboard chunk in source
# order.
_human_judge_by_variant = {"no_ai": ([], []), "ai": ([], [])}
for _t in benchathon_tasks:
    for _ev in _t.get("evaluations") or []:
        _fn = _ev.get("field_name") or ""
        if not _fn.startswith("llm_judge_falloesung") or "human:loesung" not in _fn:
            continue
        _m = _ev.get("metrics") or {}
        _rs = _m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw")
        if _rs is None: continue
        _v = variants.get(_ev.get("annotation_id"))
        if _v not in _human_judge_by_variant:
            continue
        _human_judge_by_variant[_v][0].append(_rs)
        _human_judge_by_variant[_v][1].append(bool(_m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_passed")))
```

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: only the opening sentence changes for blind mode.
Replace "We introduce the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset for
evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law.
The BenGER dataset consists of three components:" with the de-identified
form "We introduce a benchmark for evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-
based legal reasoning in German law. The dataset comprises:" — keep the
`\begin{abstract}` / `\end{abstract}` LaTeX wrappers in both modes; the
ACL formatter requires this environment, not a markdown heading. -->
```{=latex}
\begin{abstract}
```

We introduce the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset for evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. The BenGER dataset consists of three components: `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text legal case tasks across multiple levels of legal education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks. We evaluate `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems — closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight — across automatic and judge-based metrics. On a controlled validation subset of timed human-written solutions under both unaided and human–AI co-creation conditions, we contextualise model performance against these human baselines. We introduce a rubric-aligned LLM-as-a-Judge framework cross-validated against a multi-rater human-grading protocol (three blind reviews plus one author-informed creator review per solution). Our results show that replacing a blind human reviewer with the LLM judge degrades agreement with the full human pool no more than removing that reviewer altogether (Calderon r=`{python} fmt(r5_cal.get('pearson_judge_substituted_vs_full_human'),2)` vs. r=`{python} fmt(r5_cal.get('pearson_human_LOO_vs_full_human'),2)`, matched n=`{python} f"{r5_cal.get('n_pairs_judge_substituted')}"`), that closed-flagship systems lead the leaderboard across all corpora, and that human–AI co-creation substantially outperforms unaided human work.
```{=latex}
\end{abstract}
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Emit the fig-overview float here, very early in the body, so that LaTeX's
# \afterpage hook fires after page 1 ships out -- placing the figure at the
# top of page 2. The matplotlib PDF itself is generated by the chunk further
# down (after the Introduction); chunk execution order is source order, but
# the PDF file is on disk before LaTeX runs either way. Keep this chunk in
# sync with the figure builder below.
print(
    r"\afterpage{%" "\n"
    r"\begin{figure*}[t!]" "\n"
    r"\centering" "\n"
    r"\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{assets/fig-overview.png}" "\n"
    r"\caption{Overview of the benchmark and evaluation pipeline. "
    r"Three German-law corpora -- the published ZJS exam corpus, the short "
    r"Doctrinal Principles question set, and the controlled Benchathon "
    r"validation subset with human solutions in two working conditions -- "
    r"are answered by a panel of LLM systems and scored by a rubric-aligned "
    r"LLM judge. The Benchathon subset is additionally graded by a blind "
    r"human reviewer pool (with one author-informed creator review per "
    r"solution); the resulting human distribution is used to validate the "
    r"LLM judge.}" "\n"
    r"\label{fig-overview}" "\n"
    r"\end{figure*}%" "\n"
    r"}" "\n"
)
```

# Introduction

Evaluating legal reasoning is hard. Grading of legal exam answers exhibits substantial variability even among human experts: Hufeld [@hufeld2024] shows that identical answers in German law exams receive markedly different scores depending on the grader, reflecting the interpretive nature of doctrinal legal analysis. This poses a challenge for recent work on large language models (LLMs): if human evaluation itself is noisy, what does it mean to benchmark legal reasoning performance?

Existing benchmarks for legal NLP have evolved rapidly from classification [@chalkidis2022lexglue; @guha2023legalbench] to reasoning capabilities [@fei2024lawbench; @fan2026lexam; @jia2025readyjuristone; @shi2026plawbench] and retrieval-augmented evaluation [@pipitone2024legalbenchrag; @butler2026legalragbench], but most emphasise short-form QA, common-law jurisdictions, or stable ground-truth labels — leaving open-ended subsumption-based German legal reasoning under noisy expert judgments under-served.

We address these gaps with a benchmark centered on subsumption-based legal reasoning, the core reasoning paradigm in German law (@sec-legal-methodology).

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace this paragraph with the de-identified form:
"We present a benchmark^[Full code and dataset will be released upon paper
acceptance.] for evaluating LLMs on subsumption-based legal reasoning in
German law. The dataset comprises `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"`
exam-style free-text legal case tasks across different stages of legal
education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks. The
benchmark also incorporates human baselines for traditional legal work and
human–AI co-creation on a controlled Benchathon validation subset.
`Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} summarises the three corpora and the
LLM-generation-plus-evaluation pipeline."
(Drop the "BenGER" name, drop the GitHub URL, drop the platform
self-citation sentence.) -->
We present the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset^[Full code and dataset are available at <https://github.com/SebastianNagl/benger-platform>.] for evaluating LLMs on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. The BenGER dataset consists of three components covering `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"` exam-style free-text legal case tasks across different stages of legal education and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning tasks, and incorporates human baselines for traditional legal work and human–AI co-creation on a controlled Benchathon validation subset. `Figure~\ref{fig-overview}`{=latex} summarises the three components and the LLM-generation-plus-evaluation pipeline. Generation, annotation, and evaluation were run end-to-end on the BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger], the open-source web application we previously developed for benchmarking legal-domain LLM systems.

To evaluate free-text legal reasoning under noisy human judgments, we adopt a hybrid framework: an LLM-as-a-Judge aligned with German grading rubrics, cross-validated against three blind human reviews per solution and an author-informed creator review as a reference signal. We treat evaluation as a distribution over plausible expert assessments rather than a single deterministic label.

Our contributions are threefold: (i) we introduce a benchmark for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law, (ii) we provide an empirical evaluation of `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems — closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight — alongside human and co-creative baselines, and (iii) an evaluation methodology that accounts for the inherent variability of legal grading.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: drop "of the full BenGER dataset" so the sentence
reads "We organise the empirical analysis around five questions." -->
We organise the empirical analysis of the full BenGER dataset around five questions. **RQ1 (Performance):** how well do contemporary LLM systems solve open-ended subsumption-based German legal reasoning tasks, and how do they compare to human baselines? **RQ2 (System-tier effects):** how does performance vary across system tiers and open-/closed-weight access modes? **RQ3 (Evaluation validity):** how well do generic automatic metrics correlate with rubric-based legal evaluation? **RQ4 (Human–AI co-creation):** does AI-assisted drafting improve human performance, and how does it compare to standalone LLM systems? **RQ5 (Grading reliability):** how closely do LLM-judge evaluations align with the empirical distribution of human grades, and are their deviations comparable to inter-rater variability?

# Subsumption-based legal reasoning {#sec-legal-methodology}

Subsumption-based legal reasoning is the core reasoning paradigm of German civil-law doctrine [@larenz1995methodenlehre; @moellers2023methodenlehre]. Unlike common-law systems, where reasoning proceeds primarily through analogy to precedent, German legal analysis derives outcomes from codified statutory norms via a structured four-step scaffold — *Obersatz* (the legal claim or norm hypothesis to be examined), *Definition* (interpretation of the norm's elements), *Subsumtion* (anchoring of the concrete facts under those elements), and *Ergebnis* (the resulting legal consequence). This *Gutachtenstil* register — distinct from the result-first *Urteilsstil* used in judicial decisions — is the form in which German legal education, state examinations, and entry-level legal practice are trained and assessed; grading targets the written *Falllösung* end-to-end rather than the final answer alone, because the rubric rewards how each norm element is anchored in the specific fact pattern. This makes subsumption-based reasoning a structurally demanding generation task: a system has to identify the governing norm, articulate its elements, anchor every element in the facts, and conclude — a chain in which surface fluency can mask a missing *Subsumtion* step. It also makes the task unusually amenable to rubric-based evaluation, because each step in the scaffold maps to an identifiable component of the generated answer rather than to a global quality impression. The English-language legal NLP benchmarks centered on classification, multiple-choice, or precedent-style reasoning [@chalkidis2022lexglue; @guha2023legalbench] do not exercise this scaffold, leaving German subsumption-based legal reasoning under noisy expert judgement under-served.

# Related Work

Legal NLP benchmarking has evolved from classification baselines — LexGLUE [@chalkidis2022lexglue] (seven English legal datasets) and LegalBench [@guha2023legalbench] (162 mostly short-form US-federal-law tasks) — to reasoning-centric, jurisdiction-specific evaluation. Recent work targets jurisdiction-specific reasoning: LawBench [@fei2024lawbench] and LexEval [@li2024lexeval] for Chinese civil law, KBL [@kim2024kbl] for Korean bar-exam questions, LAR-ECHR [@chlapanis2024larechr] for European Court of Human Rights argument chains, KOBLEX [@lee2025koblex] for provision-grounded multi-hop reasoning in Korean law, GreekBarBench [@chlapanis2025greekbarbench] for free-text Greek bar exams, NitiBench [@akarajaradwong2025nitibench] for Thai financial and tax law, PLawBench [@shi2026plawbench] for rubric-graded Chinese legal practice across 13 practical scenarios, and LaborBench [@hariri2025laborbench] for US state labor-law statutory simplification. A complementary line targets retrieval-augmented evaluation — LegalBench-RAG [@pipitone2024legalbenchrag] and Legal RAG Bench [@butler2026legalragbench] — and discrepancy-based auditing of LLM legal reasoning — CLAUSE [@choudhury2026clause]. The closest precedent is LEXam [@fan2026lexam] — 7,537 questions across 340 law exams in English and German, with an ensemble LLM-as-a-Judge validated against human experts — but its rubric is not the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* scaffold central to German *Falllösung* grading (@sec-legal-methodology) and it does not couple its free-text items to the dimension-level grading criteria used in German law school and state-exam evaluation.

Evaluating free-text legal reasoning relies increasingly on LLM-as-a-Judge approaches, which raises concerns over bias, calibration, and alignment with expert judgment. Research in German legal education shows that human grading itself is substantially variable: Hufeld [@hufeld2024] reports an experiment with 15 beginner-level law exams and 23 graders, in which the same submissions received 15 or 16 independent grades each — the average range between the lowest and highest grade per answer was 6.47 points on the German 0–18 scale, and only 42% of grades fell within ±1 point of the answer-specific mean. Evaluation in legal reasoning tasks is therefore better treated as a distribution over plausible expert judgments than as a single ground truth, motivating hybrid methodologies that combine human expertise with calibrated LLM-based scoring.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "our prior BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger]
provides … workflows on which the present dataset was built." with the
non-self-attributing form "the BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger] provides
an end-to-end framework for German legal case analysis and
subsumption-based reasoning workflows.", and replace "The BenGER dataset
addresses this gap" with "Our benchmark addresses this gap". -->
Resources for German legal NLP remain limited: GerLayQA [@buttner2024gerlayqa] provides ~21,000 lay questions paired with lawyer answers but focuses on QA rather than subsumption-based reasoning, GerLeRB [@weber2025gerlerb] targets legislative retrieval, and our prior BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger] provides an end-to-end framework for German legal case analysis and subsumption-based reasoning workflows on which the present dataset was built. German legal reasoning grounded in doctrinal subsumption under statutory law remains under-served, especially for open-ended, structured analysis under noisy human judgments. The BenGER dataset addresses this gap by combining a `{python} f"{n_zjs + n_benchathon}"`-task exam-style corpus and `{python} f"{n_qa}"` short doctrinal reasoning items with a controlled Benchathon validation subset, a German doctrinal rubric, and a multi-rater cross-validation protocol.

# Dataset

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: "The BenGER dataset draws on three components" →
"Our benchmark draws on three corpora". -->
The BenGER dataset draws on three components covering the main forms of legal reasoning in German legal education: two **exam-style case** corpora in which a fact pattern is given and a structured *Falllösung* is expected, and one set of **short doctrinal reasoning** items that probes core principles without the full case-analysis scaffolding.

The ZJS corpus contributes `{python} n_zjs` publicly available exam-style cases from the *Zeitschrift für das Juristische Studium*^[<https://www.zjs-online.com/>], covering civil, criminal, and public law and spanning early undergraduate exercises through first and selected second state-examination materials, each paired with an expert-written reference solution. The Benchathon corpus adds `{python} n_benchathon` newly collected exam-style tasks at an intermediate difficulty level (more demanding than introductory exercises, less complex than full first-state-exam problems), each with `{python} n_human_total` human solutions (`{python} n_human_only` traditional, `{python} n_co_creation` human–AI co-creation).^[The corpus is named after the in-person Benchathon event at which participants produced these solutions and the validation human grading. Participants were predominantly law students at various stages of legal training, plus a smaller group of *Referendare* (post-state-exam legal trainees), recent graduates, and a small layperson cohort; full composition in Appendix `\ref{benchathon-participant-composition}`{=latex}.] The Doctrinal Principles corpus (*Grundprinzipien*) contributes `{python} n_qa` short doctrinal reasoning items (question–answer pairs with explanatory reasoning) on core principles, with recent case law where appropriate. ZJS supports at-scale automated evaluation; Benchathon supports the human baseline + judge-validation layer; Doctrinal Principles probes the same reasoning at shorter form.

The benchmark is intended for evaluation only; we do not define a train/test split, since the objective is comparative evaluation of deployed LLM systems and human baselines. Pretraining-contamination considerations across the three corpora are discussed in Limitations §5.

## Benchathon task design and human solution collection

Benchathon participants solved tasks in a controlled annotation environment, with each task randomly assigned to one of two conditions: in the *traditional* condition, participants could use statutory texts, legal databases, literature, and internet research; in the *co-creation* condition, AI tools could additionally be used. Only the final written solution is evaluated, mirroring legal examination practice; auxiliary materials (notes, outlines, span markings) were retained for future analysis but are not used here (Appendix `\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}). Each task had a two-hour time limit; participant expertise was measured objectively (reported legal grades and qualifications) and subjectively (self-assessed competence on a Likert scale).

# Experimental setup

## Generation

We evaluate `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` contemporary LLM systems covering closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight reference systems: `{python} f"{sum(1 for s in (systems_meta or []) if s.get('openness') == 'closed')}"` closed-weight via provider APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) and `{python} f"{sum(1 for s in (systems_meta or []) if s.get('openness') == 'open')}"` open-weight via a managed inference provider (DeepInfra). Per-system providers, snapshots, observed temperatures, token-budget settings, truncation counts, and the short aliases used in the body are in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex} (Appendix C). Each task received at least one generation per system; flagship and large-context systems received additional repetitions where capacity allowed. The Benchathon subset was generated by all systems; on ZJS, all `{python} f"{len(zjs_summary) if zjs_summary else 12}"` systems produced coverage of up to `{python} f"{max((r['n_tasks'] for r in zjs_summary), default=n_zjs)}"` published exams.^[Individual questions were occasionally flagged as prohibited content by the provider's safety filter, leaving a small handful of per-system coverage gaps below the corpus total.]

All systems were prompted with the same German-language system and instruction prompts (Appendix `\ref{system-prompts-for-generation}`{=latex} + `\ref{instruction-prompts-for-generation}`{=latex}) and configured to return a single JSON object containing the full *Falllösung*, with the instruction prompt mirroring the *Gutachtenstil* scaffold (@sec-legal-methodology). Provider-recommended temperatures and token budgets were used (temperature 0.0–1.0, max output 8,000–16,000 tokens); generations malformed or shorter than 200 output tokens after a bounded retry budget were excluded. Truncation in kept generations is concentrated in the smaller open-weight systems (Trunc. column, `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex}).

# Evaluation

## Black-box system evaluation
We evaluate LLM systems through provider APIs in a black-box setting, so our results measure end-to-end system behavior rather than isolated base-model capability. We do not enable retrieval or tool-use endpoints; components we cannot disable — notably content filters and, on some products, internal routing between sub-models — operate by default. This reflects realistic deployment conditions but limits causal claims about underlying architectures.

## Automatic metrics

Per generation we compute BLEU [@papineni2002bleu], ROUGE [@lin2004rouge], METEOR [@banerjee2005meteor], chrF, BERTScore [@zhang2020bertscore], MoverScore [@zhao2019moverscore], and a Sentence-BERT cosine similarity [@reimers2019sentencebert] against the expert reference solution. Per-system means appear in the main leaderboard tables; per-generation values are correlated against the LLM-judge rubric score in RQ3.


## LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation
For rubric-based automatic evaluation, we use a specialized LLM-as-a-Judge prompt aligned with German legal grading practices. The judge receives the task, the reference solution, and the solution to be evaluated. It assigns scores across ten dimensions covering result correctness (*Ergebnisrichtigkeit*), issue identification (*Vollständigkeit*), legal grounding (*Rechtsgrundlagen*), doctrinal knowledge (*Rechtskenntnis*), subsumption quality (*Subsumtion*), problem depth (*Schwerpunktsetzung*), methodological structure (*Methodischer Stil*), organization (*Gliederung*), terminology (*Sprache*), and formal correctness (*Formalia*). Dimension scores are aggregated to a 100-point raw score and mapped to the German 0–18 grade-point scale. A generation counts as a *pass* when its grade-point score reaches *ausreichend* (≥ 4 on the 0–18 scale, corresponding to a raw score of ≥ 50/100), following the German *Staatsexamen* (state law examination) convention; this is the boolean reported in the *Pass* columns of `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} and in the pass-rate aggregates throughout. The rubric operationalises the *Gutachtenstil* scaffold (@sec-legal-methodology): each step in the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* schema is reflected in a dedicated dimension (issue identification, legal grounding, doctrinal knowledge, subsumption quality, methodological structure), so the per-dimension scores in @fig-rubric-dim correspond to identifiable components of the reasoning process.

The judge is blind to solution origin and receives inputs in a fixed (task, reference, candidate) order. The primary judge is GPT-5-mini; configuration in Appendix `\ref{llm-judge-configuration}`{=latex}, prompt in Appendix `\ref{evaluation-prompts-for-the-llm-judge}`{=latex}. Since judge-based evaluation may itself be model-dependent, we cross-validate the judge against a multi-rater human-grading layer on a Benchathon subset (design below; results in RQ5). To quantify judge stability we additionally run two complementary checks on the Benchathon subset — *within-judge stochasticity* (k=3 re-runs of GPT-5-mini) and *between-judge bias* (additional evaluations by Opus-4.7 and Gemini-3.1-Pro); both are reported in RQ5. Outputs conform to a predefined JSON schema; non-conforming outputs are retried and reported as evaluation failures if they cannot be parsed.

## Human Evaluation
Human evaluation is conducted on a controlled subset of 45 Benchathon solutions: one traditional human, one human–AI co-created, and one LLM-generated solution per Benchathon task. Human submissions are selected as the median-expertise participant per legal area (*Bereich*), where expertise is a z-score combining self-assessed competence and, where available, first-state-exam grade (details in Appendix `\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}); LLM submissions are drawn through a tier-balanced rotation across closed-flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight reference systems. Each selected solution receives three independent blind reviews and one author-informed creator review (180 reviews total). Blind reviewers grade against the same rubric as the LLM judge and form the IRR pool used to validate the judge; creator reviews use the same rubric but are not pooled into IRR since task authors have privileged knowledge of the intended solution. The full selection and reviewer-assignment procedure is given in Appendix `\ref{full-benchathon-human-evaluation-assignment-procedure}`{=latex}; the agreement statistics — Spearman / Pearson / MAE / Cohen's $\kappa$ [@cohen1960coefficient] / ICC(2,1) / ICC(2,k) [@shrout1979intraclass; @koo2016guideline] / Calderon alternative-annotator [@calderon2025] — are reported alongside their values in RQ5. The central question is not whether the LLM judge reproduces a single human grade, but whether its deviation is comparable to that among human graders.

# Results

Unless otherwise noted, scores in this section are the LLM judge's rubric scores, as 0–100 raw points or 0–18 German grade points. The human-grading layer covers the 45-pick validation design with one author-informed creator review and three blind reviews per pick (180 reviews total). The analysis pipeline regenerates every reported number from the data file checked in alongside the manuscript.

## RQ1: Overall performance of LLM systems and humans

The unified per-system view across the three corpora is shown in `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}; per-corpus full leaderboards with surface-metric columns are in `Appendix~\ref{full-results-table}`{=latex}. On the Benchathon subset, top-tier closed systems score in the low- to mid-80s on the 0–100 judge scale, with Sonnet-4.6 (`{python} fmt(b('claude-sonnet-4-6'),1)`), Opus-4.7 (`{python} fmt(b('claude-opus-4-7'),1)`), and GPT-5.4 (`{python} fmt(b('gpt-5.4'),1)`) clustered at the top with pass rates near 1.0, followed by Gemini-3.1-Pro (`{python} fmt(b('gemini-3.1-pro-preview'),1)`) in the upper 70s. The flagship open-weight systems DeepSeek-V4-Pro and Qwen3-235B trail the top closed models by roughly `{python} fmt(b('claude-sonnet-4-6') - b('deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro'),0)`–`{python} fmt(b('claude-sonnet-4-6') - b('Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507'),0)` raw points. The same ordering broadly reproduces on ZJS (`Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}, ZJS columns), where GPT-5.4 leads at `{python} fmt(z('gpt-5.4'),1)` raw points (`{python} fmt(z('gpt-5.4','grade_points_mean'),1)` grade points, `{python} fmtpct(z('gpt-5.4','pass_rate'),0)` pass) and the weakest open-weight Llama-4 trails at `{python} fmt(z('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8'),1)` raw points (`{python} fmt(z('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8','grade_points_mean'),1)` grade points, `{python} fmtpct(z('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8','pass_rate'),0)` pass).

The per-task heatmap (@fig-task-system-heatmap, `Appendix~\ref{per-task-per-system-heatmap}`{=latex}) shows that the leaderboard ordering hides substantial within-task variance: top-tier systems hold a per-task standard deviation of 6–8 raw points, while mid-tier and smaller open-weight systems show 10–15 — roughly twice the spread for the same overall mean. On the Benchathon subset, the human cohort scores in the mid-range of the LLM distribution under both conditions, with the co-creation condition shifted upward (see human rows in `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex} and @fig-human-vs-ai in RQ4). The judge–human–LLM comparison rests on a single judge model (see Limitations).

On the short-form Doctrinal Principles corpus (`Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}, Doctrinal Principles columns; `{python} f"{len(grundprinzipien_summary)}"` systems, `{python} f"{sum(r['n_generations'] for r in grundprinzipien_summary)}"` generations under a 4-dimension rubric), the same flagship cluster leads: Sonnet-4.6 (`{python} fmt(g('claude-sonnet-4-6'),1)`), Gemini-3.1-Pro (`{python} fmt(g('gemini-3.1-pro-preview'),1)`), Opus-4.7 (`{python} fmt(g('claude-opus-4-7'),1)`), GPT-5.4 (`{python} fmt(g('gpt-5.4'),1)`). Doctrinal Principles items carry a Ja/Nein decision label; across systems accuracy ranges from `{python} fmtpct(min((r['metrics'].get('accuracy') or 0) for r in grundprinzipien_summary), 0)` to `{python} fmtpct(max((r['metrics'].get('accuracy') or 0) for r in grundprinzipien_summary), 0)`, with Gemini-3.1-Pro at `{python} fmtpct(g('gemini-3.1-pro-preview','accuracy'),0)` and Opus-4.7 at `{python} fmtpct(g('claude-opus-4-7','accuracy'),0)`.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
# Table helpers (_tex_escape, emit_tex_table) and a few formatting maps used
# by both this body chunk and downstream appendix chunks. Defined here so the
# unified main leaderboard chunk below — which sits inside the Results
# section to anchor the table* float near RQ1/RQ2 — can use them without
# depending on the deferred chunks at the end of the document.
def _fmt(v):
    if v is None: return "—"
    if isinstance(v, float):
        return f"{v:.2g}" if abs(v) < 1 else f"{v:.0f}"
    return str(v)

_tier_label = {"flagship": "Flagship", "efficiency": "Efficiency",
               "open_reference": "Open ref."}
_access_label = {"provider_api": "Provider API",
                 "managed_inference": "Managed inf."}


def _tex_escape(s):
    """Minimal LaTeX escape for cells with text from the dataset."""
    if s is None:
        return "---"
    s = str(s)
    replacements = (
        ("\\", r"\textbackslash{}"),
        ("&", r"\&"), ("%", r"\%"), ("$", r"\$"), ("#", r"\#"),
        ("_", r"\_"), ("{", r"\{"), ("}", r"\}"),
        ("~", r"\textasciitilde{}"), ("^", r"\textasciicircum{}"),
        ("—", "---"), ("–", "--"),
    )
    for a, b in replacements:
        s = s.replace(a, b)
    return s


def emit_tex_table(label, caption, headers, rows, *,
                   col_spec=None, span_two_columns=False,
                   first_col_left=True, tex_escape=True,
                   tabcolsep_pt=4, fontsize_cmd=r"\footnotesize",
                   placement="t", fill_width=False, shrink_to_fit=False):
    """Print a native LaTeX float wrapping a booktabs tabular.

    fill_width=True switches the inner environment from ``tabular`` to
    ``tabular*`` with ``@{\\extracolsep{\\fill}}`` so the table stretches to
    the full text-column width of the float (\\columnwidth / \\textwidth)
    without rescaling fonts.

    shrink_to_fit=True wraps the tabular in ``\\resizebox{<width>}{!}{...}``
    so over-wide tables shrink to the float width instead of bleeding into
    the right margin. Forces a plain ``tabular`` (not ``tabular*``) because
    ``\\resizebox`` can't take an explicit-width tabular* as its argument.
    Use this for appendix tables with many numeric columns.
    """
    n_cols = len(headers)
    if col_spec is None:
        col_spec = ("l" if first_col_left else "c") + "c" * (n_cols - 1)
    env = "table*" if span_two_columns else "table"
    target_w = r"\textwidth" if span_two_columns else r"\columnwidth"
    out = [
        f"\\begin{{{env}}}[{placement}]",
        r"\centering" + fontsize_cmd,
        r"\caption{" + caption + "}",
        f"\\label{{{label}}}",
        f"\\setlength{{\\tabcolsep}}{{{tabcolsep_pt}pt}}",
    ]
    if shrink_to_fit:
        out.append(f"\\resizebox{{{target_w}}}{{!}}{{%")
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular}}{{{col_spec}}}")
    elif fill_width:
        col_spec_filled = "@{\\extracolsep{\\fill}}" + col_spec + "@{}"
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular*}}{{{target_w}}}{{{col_spec_filled}}}")
    else:
        out.append(f"\\begin{{tabular}}{{{col_spec}}}")
    out += [
        r"\toprule",
        " & ".join(headers) + r" \\",
        r"\midrule",
    ]
    for r in rows:
        cells = [(_tex_escape(c) if tex_escape else (c if c is not None else "---"))
                 for c in r]
        out.append(" & ".join(cells) + r" \\")
    out.append(r"\bottomrule")
    if shrink_to_fit:
        out.append(r"\end{tabular}")
        out.append(r"}%  end \resizebox")
    else:
        out.append(r"\end{tabular*}" if fill_width else r"\end{tabular}")
    out.append(f"\\end{{{env}}}")
    out.append("")
    print("\n".join(out))
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

# Unified main leaderboard across the three corpora. Sits in the Results
# section (between RQ1 and RQ2) so the table* float lands near its first
# reference. Replaces the previously separate per-corpus body tables; the
# surface-metric-rich per-corpus leaderboards live in Appendix~E. The two
# Benchathon human-baseline rows are pinned at the bottom.

import math, random, statistics
from collections import defaultdict

# Raw cells in the table are rendered as "mean ± half-CI" where half-CI is the
# half-width of a 95% interval on the per-system mean. Two derivations:
#   - Benchathon: bootstrap CI from per-generation raw scores in model_evals.
#   - ZJS / Grundprinzipien: analytic CI = 1.96 · stdev / sqrt(n) using the
#     per-system stdev added to the summary JSONs by derive_zjs_summary.py and
#     derive_grundprinzipien_summary.py (Welford's online algorithm).

random.seed(7)

def _bench_boot_ci(raws, n_boot=2000):
    """Half-width of a 95% bootstrap CI on the mean."""
    if len(raws) < 3:
        return None
    samples = [statistics.mean(random.choices(raws, k=len(raws)))
               for _ in range(n_boot)]
    samples.sort()
    lo = samples[int(0.025 * n_boot)]
    hi = samples[int(0.975 * n_boot) - 1]
    return (hi - lo) / 2.0

def _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n):
    """Half-width of a 95% Gaussian CI on the mean (1.96 · SE)."""
    if mean is None or stdev is None or n is None or n <= 1:
        return None
    return 1.96 * stdev / math.sqrt(n)

_bench_by_sys_full = defaultdict(list)
for r in (model_evals or []):
    _bench_by_sys_full[r["system"]].append(r)

def _bench_agg(sys_id):
    rs = _bench_by_sys_full.get(sys_id) or []
    raws = [x["raw_score"] for x in rs if x.get("raw_score") is not None]
    ps   = [bool(x["passed"]) for x in rs if x.get("passed") is not None]
    return (len(raws) if raws else None,
            statistics.mean(raws) if raws else None,
            _bench_boot_ci(raws),
            (sum(ps) / len(ps)) if ps else None)

_zjs_by_sys_full = {r["system"]: r for r in (zjs_summary or [])}
def _zjs_agg(sys_id):
    r = _zjs_by_sys_full.get(sys_id)
    if not r: return (None, None, None, None)
    m = r.get("metrics") or {}
    mean = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_mean")
    stdev = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_stdev")
    n_w = m.get("llm_judge_falloesung_raw_n") or r.get("n_generations")
    return (r.get("n_generations"), mean,
            _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n_w),
            m.get("pass_rate"))

_gp_by_sys_full = {r["system"]: r for r in (grundprinzipien_summary or [])}
def _gp_agg(sys_id):
    r = _gp_by_sys_full.get(sys_id)
    if not r: return (None, None, None, None, None)
    m = r.get("metrics") or {}
    mean = m.get("judge_raw_mean")
    stdev = m.get("judge_raw_stdev")
    n_w = m.get("judge_raw_n") or r.get("n_generations")
    return (r.get("n_generations"), mean,
            _analytic_ci_half(mean, stdev, n_w),
            m.get("judge_pass_rate"),
            m.get("accuracy"))

_all_sys_ids = set(_bench_by_sys_full) | set(_zjs_by_sys_full) | set(_gp_by_sys_full)

def _mean_avail(sys_id):
    vals = []
    for fn in (_bench_agg, _zjs_agg, _gp_agg):
        v = fn(sys_id)
        if v[1] is not None: vals.append(v[1])
    return statistics.mean(vals) if vals else 0.0

_sys_order = sorted(_all_sys_ids, key=lambda s: -_mean_avail(s))

def _raw_ci(mean, half):
    """Compact 'mean ± half' cell; falls back to '---' if mean missing."""
    if mean is None:
        return "---"
    if half is None:
        return f"{mean:.1f}"
    return f"{mean:.1f}\\,$\\pm$\\,{half:.1f}"

def _pct(v):  return "---" if v is None else f"{100*v:.0f}\\%"
def _n(v):    return "---" if v is None else str(v)

_rows = []
for sys_id in _sys_order:
    bn, br, br_ci, bp = _bench_agg(sys_id)
    zn, zr, zr_ci, zp = _zjs_agg(sys_id)
    gn, gr_, gr_ci, gp_r, ga = _gp_agg(sys_id)
    _rows.append([
        _tex_escape(alias_for(sys_id)),
        _n(bn), _raw_ci(br, br_ci), _pct(bp),
        _n(zn), _raw_ci(zr, zr_ci), _pct(zp),
        _n(gn), _raw_ci(gr_, gr_ci), _pct(gp_r), _pct(ga),
    ])

def _human_row(label, raws, passed):
    if not raws:
        return [label, "0", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---"]
    return [label, str(len(raws)),
            _raw_ci(statistics.mean(raws), _bench_boot_ci(raws)),
            f"{100*sum(passed)/len(passed):.0f}\\%",
            "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---", "---"]

_human_all_raw  = _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0] + _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0]
_human_all_pass = _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][1] + _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][1]

_lines = [
    r"\begin{table*}[t]",
    r"\centering\footnotesize",
    "\\caption{Main leaderboard across the three corpora. Per-system mean "
    "LLM-judge raw score (0--100, mean\\,$\\pm$\\,half-width of a 95\\% CI: "
    "percentile bootstrap $B=2000$ on Benchathon for small per-system $n$, "
    "analytic 1.96\\,$\\cdot$\\,SE on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles) and pass "
    "rate; Doctrinal Principles also reports Ja/Nein decision accuracy. "
    "Systems are ranked by the mean of their available per-corpus raw scores. "
    "The two human-baseline rows use the LLM judge's grades of human-written "
    "Benchathon solutions (traditional = unaided; co-creation = human--AI "
    "co-creation). Per-system 95\\% CIs are in "
    "Appendix~\\ref{full-results-table}.}",
    r"\label{tbl-main-leaderboard}",
    r"\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}",
    r"\begin{tabular*}{\textwidth}{@{\extracolsep{\fill}} l rrr rrr rrrr @{}}",
    r"\toprule",
    r" & \multicolumn{3}{c}{Benchathon}"
    r" & \multicolumn{3}{c}{ZJS}"
    r" & \multicolumn{4}{c}{Doctrinal Principles} \\",
    r"\cmidrule(lr){2-4} \cmidrule(lr){5-7} \cmidrule(lr){8-11}",
    r"System / Group & $n$ & Raw & Pass"
    r" & $n$ & Raw & Pass"
    r" & $n$ & Raw & Pass & Acc. \\",
    r"\midrule",
]
for row in _rows:
    _lines.append(" & ".join(row) + r" \\")

_lines.append(r"\midrule")
for label, raws, passed in [
    ("Human (all)",       _human_all_raw, _human_all_pass),
    (r"\quad traditional", _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0], _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][1]),
    (r"\quad co-creation", _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0],    _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][1]),
]:
    _lines.append(" & ".join(_human_row(label, raws, passed)) + r" \\")

_lines += [r"\bottomrule", r"\end{tabular*}", r"\end{table*}", ""]
print("\n".join(_lines))
```

## RQ2: Scaling and system effects

Closed flagship systems from OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google occupy the top of every per-corpus leaderboard, while efficiency-tier closed systems and the strongest open-weight systems (DeepSeek-V4-Pro, Qwen3-235B) form an overlapping mid-band — closed-API dominance narrows substantially below the flagship tier. Aggregating by tier, the flagship-vs-open-weight gap is `{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points on Benchathon and narrows to `{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_tier','open_reference','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` on ZJS; the closed-vs-open weights gap shrinks similarly from `{python} f"{(_ta('benchathon','by_weights','closed','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('benchathon','by_weights','open','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` to `{python} f"{(_ta('zjs','by_weights','closed','mean_raw') or 0) - (_ta('zjs','by_weights','open','mean_raw') or 0):.0f}"` raw points. The same direction holds on Doctrinal Principles, so the flagship-vs-open-weight ordering survives the three different task formats and the 4-dimension rubric variant. We frame these as system-level rather than model-level effects, since black-box API access entangles the open–closed split with provider-side prompts, decoding, post-processing, and possible retrieval or tool use (see Limitations).

## RQ3: Evaluation validity — automatic metric correlation

Across all three corpora, lexical surface metrics correlate with the LLM judge's rubric raw score more strongly than embedding-based metrics, running partly against the common expectation that semantic-embedding metrics dominate lexical ones on long-form legal text. On Benchathon, METEOR (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('meteor',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), ROUGE (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('rouge',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), and BLEU (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bleu',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) lead while MoverScore (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('moverscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), BERTScore (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('bertscore',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`), and sentence-embedding similarity (r=`{python} fmtp(r3.get('semantic_similarity',{}).get('pearson_vs_judge_raw'))`) trail; the same ordering broadly holds on ZJS and Doctrinal Principles (`Appendix~\ref{automatic-metric-vs.-llm-judge-correlation}`{=latex}: `Table~\ref{tbl-metric-correlation}`{=latex}, @fig-metric-correlation). This is consistent with the highly templated structure of doctrinal German exam writing, where reference and high-quality system answers share substantial n-gram material around statutory citations and the *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* scaffolding. At the per-system level on Benchathon, the lexical metrics' Spearman against the judge ranking reaches +`{python} fmt(_rc('benchathon','meteor'))` (METEOR) while BERTScore (+`{python} fmt(_rc('benchathon','bertscore'))`) and semantic similarity (+`{python} fmt(_rc('benchathon','semantic_similarity'))`) trail — a leaderboard built on a strong lexical metric would broadly preserve the judge's coarse-tier ordering but disagree on the mid-tier, motivating a calibrated LLM judge as the primary scoring instrument.

<!-- tbl-metric-correlation moved to Appendix I -->

## RQ4: Human–AI co-creation under LLM-judge scoring

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-human-vs-ai
#| fig-cap: "Mean LLM-judge raw score (0–100) per group with 95% bootstrap CIs on the Benchathon validation subset. Human bars: judge grades of human-written solutions, split by working condition (traditional = unaided; co-creation = human–AI co-creation). AI bars: per-generation judge scores pooled across closed-flagship-tier systems only (flagship) and across all evaluated LLM systems (pooled)."
#| fig-pos: 't'

# Single-column figure (Quarto-native crossref via @fig-human-vs-ai). Compact
# four-group bar chart sized for a single column of the ACL two-column
# layout (~3.3 in wide). The flagship-only AI bar makes the co-creation ≈
# closed-flagship-tier comparison visually immediate.

import random, statistics
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

random.seed(11)

def _hvsa_boot_ci(xs, n=2000):
    xs = [float(x) for x in xs if x is not None]
    if len(xs) < 3:
        return (statistics.mean(xs) if xs else 0.0, 0.0, 0.0)
    pt = statistics.mean(xs)
    samples = sorted(statistics.mean(random.choices(xs, k=len(xs))) for _ in range(n))
    return pt, samples[int(0.025 * n)], samples[int(0.975 * n) - 1]

_flagship_ids = {s["model_id"] for s in (systems_meta or []) if s.get("tier") == "flagship"}
_flagship_raws = [r["raw_score"] for r in (model_evals or [])
                  if r.get("raw_score") is not None and r.get("system") in _flagship_ids]

_groups = [
    ("Human\ntraditional", _human_judge_by_variant["no_ai"][0]),
    ("Human\nco-creation", _human_judge_by_variant["ai"][0]),
]
if model_evals:
    _ai_raws = [r["raw_score"] for r in model_evals if r["raw_score"] is not None]
    _groups.append(("AI all\n(pooled)", _ai_raws))
if _flagship_raws:
    _groups.append(("AI flagship\n(closed)", _flagship_raws))

_labels = [g[0] for g in _groups]
_points, _los, _his = zip(*(_hvsa_boot_ci(g[1]) for g in _groups))
_ns = [len(g[1]) for g in _groups]

_fig, _ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(2.95, 1.9))
_xs = range(len(_groups))
_ax.bar(_xs, _points,
        yerr=[[p - l for p, l in zip(_points, _los)],
              [h - p for p, h in zip(_points, _his)]],
        color=["#6c8eb0", "#b08056", "#a89bc8", "#6f5fa0"][:len(_groups)],
        capsize=2.5, edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.4, width=0.55)
for _x, _p, _n in zip(_xs, _points, _ns):
    _ax.text(_x, _p + 1.5, f"{_p:.1f}", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=6.5, weight="bold")
    _ax.text(_x, 2, f"$n$={_n}", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=5.8, color="#444")
_ax.set_xticks(list(_xs))
_ax.set_xticklabels(_labels, fontsize=6.5)
_ax.set_ylabel("LLM-judge raw (0–100)", fontsize=6.5)
_ax.tick_params(axis="y", labelsize=6)
_ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
_ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
_ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle=":", linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.6)
_ax.set_axisbelow(True)
plt.tight_layout(pad=0.2)
plt.show()
```

The LLM judge rates co-creation solutions substantially higher than traditional-condition solutions on the Benchathon (@fig-human-vs-ai; `Table~\ref{tbl-main-leaderboard}`{=latex}). The mean judge raw score is `{python} fmt(r4.get('mean_traditional'),1)` on the `{python} f"{r4.get('n_traditional')}"` traditional-condition solutions versus `{python} fmt(r4.get('mean_co_creation'),1)` on the `{python} f"{r4.get('n_co_creation')}"` co-creation solutions, a mean difference of `{python} fmtp(r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'),1)` raw points (bootstrap 95% CI: [`{python} fmtp(r4.get('ci95_lo'),1)`, `{python} fmtp(r4.get('ci95_hi'),1)`]). The co-creation mean lands inside the closed-flagship-tier 95% CI (mean `{python} fmt(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','mean_raw'),1)`, CI [`{python} fmt(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','ci95_lo'),1)`, `{python} fmt(_ta('benchathon','by_tier','flagship','ci95_hi'),1)`]), while the traditional-condition mean sits between mid-pack open-weight systems and the weakest open-weight Llama-4 (mean `{python} fmt(b('meta-llama/Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8'),1)`). Per-condition n is unbalanced (`{python} f"{r4.get('n_co_creation')} vs. {r4.get('n_traditional')}"`) and observations are not strictly independent across participants; the bootstrap is therefore non-clustered, and the judge–LLM-style-alignment caveat is discussed in Limitations. Per-domain bootstrap intervals (civil law, public law, criminal law) overlap, so we do not infer at the domain level.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(rq4_human_sentence)
```

## RQ5: Grading reliability

The headline of RQ5: within the per-solution variability humans already show on the same answers, the LLM judge can substitute for a reviewer without degrading the pool's agreement with the full-human reference. The same picture holds across model lineages: judges from three different families (GPT-5-mini, Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro) disagree with each other by an amount comparable to disagreement among independent human graders, while preserving system ordering. Detailed numbers are in `Appendix~\ref{judgehuman-agreement-statistics}`{=latex} (`Table~\ref{tbl-agreement}`{=latex}); the per-dimension breakdown is in @fig-rubric-dim.

`\paragraph{Judge vs.\ the mean human grade.}`{=latex}
On the `{python} f"{r5_jh.get('n_annotations')}"` annotations where the LLM judge has a complete grade, the judge agrees with the per-solution mean of the blind human reviewers at Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'))` and Spearman ρ=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('spearman_raw'))` on raw 0–100 scores, with a mean absolute error of `{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_raw'),1)` raw points (≈`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('mae_grade_points'),1)` grade points on the 0–18 scale) and Cohen's κ=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('passfail_cohen_kappa'))` for the pass/fail decision. For context, the blind reviewers themselves disagree on the same solutions by a mean within-annotation max–min spread of `{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_raw'),1)` raw points (`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_grade_points'),1)` grade points), broadly comparable to the several-grade-point range that prior work on German legal grading reports [@hufeld2024]. As a single-number reliability summary, the blind-reviewer pool has ICC(2,k)=`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('icc_2_k_raw'))` on raw scores — *intra-class correlation coefficient*, the standard inter-rater reliability metric [@shrout1979intraclass]; we report the two-way random-effects variants ICC(2,1) and ICC(2,k), with their grade-point counterparts, in `Table~\ref{tbl-agreement}`{=latex}.

`\paragraph{Alternative-annotator substitution.}`{=latex}
A sharper test of whether the judge can stand in for a human is the Calderon-style alternative-annotator check [@calderon2025]: drop one human from the pool and either (a) substitute the LLM judge in that slot or (b) leave the slot empty, then re-aggregate the resulting pool and correlate it against the full-human reference. On the `{python} f"{r5_cal.get('n_pairs_judge_substituted')}"` matched annotations, both operations yield essentially the same correlation with the full-human pool — Pearson r=`{python} fmt(r5_cal.get('pearson_judge_substituted_vs_full_human'))` for the judge-substituted pool, r=`{python} fmt(r5_cal.get('pearson_human_LOO_vs_full_human'))` for the human leave-one-out. The judge's contribution to the per-solution pool score is therefore indistinguishable from a typical human's at this scale: substituting the judge degrades agreement with the full-human pool no more than dropping that human altogether. Both correlations are computed on the same underlying annotations, so we report this as a descriptive matched comparison rather than an inferential test.

`\paragraph{Dimension-level pattern.}`{=latex}
@fig-rubric-dim breaks judge–human agreement down by rubric dimension. Judge–human agreement is highest on dimensions with structured scoring criteria — `{python} _dim_prose_top(3)` — and lowest on dimensions tied more to presentation and form — `{python} _dim_prose_bottom(3)`; per-dimension MAE ranges from `{python} fmt(dim_mae_formalia)` (Formal correctness, max 2 points) to `{python} fmt(dim_mae_ergebnis,1)` (Result correctness, max 20 points). Per-dimension Pearson values are reported descriptively without multi-comparison correction; we do not make inferential claims about individual dimensions.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(rq5_extension)
```

`\paragraph{Judge re-run stability.}`{=latex}
Re-running the same GPT-5-mini judge k=3 times on each generation in the Benchathon validation subset (`{python} f"{r5_jr.get('n_generations_with_repeats')}"` generations) yields a mean within-cell stdev of `{python} fmt(r5_jr.get('mean_within_gen_stdev'),2)` raw points — substantially tighter than the inter-human within-solution spread of `{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_raw'),1)` raw points reported above. The judge is therefore more self-consistent across re-runs than the human raters are with each other on the same solutions.

`\paragraph{Inter-judge agreement across model families.}`{=latex}
Across three judge families (GPT-5-mini, Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro) on the same `{python} f"{inter_judge.get('n_cells_with_triplets')}"`-generation subset, judges from different model lineages disagree by an amount comparable to disagreement among independent human graders, while preserving system ordering. The underlying numbers: a clear strictness gradient — Opus-4.7 scores on average `{python} fmt((inter_judge.get('per_judge_stats') or {}).get('gpt-5-mini',{}).get('mean',0) - (inter_judge.get('per_judge_stats') or {}).get('claude-opus-4-7',{}).get('mean',0),1)` raw points lower than GPT-5-mini, with Gemini-3.1-Pro in between; pairwise rank agreement remains high (Spearman ρ between `{python} fmt(min((p.get('spearman_rho') or 1) for p in (inter_judge.get('pairwise') or {}).values()),2)` and `{python} fmt(max((p.get('spearman_rho') or 0) for p in (inter_judge.get('pairwise') or {}).values()),2)` across the three pairs); and the within-cell stdev across the three judges is `{python} fmt((inter_judge.get('within_cell_stdev') or {}).get('median'),1)` raw points (median), on the same order as the inter-human spread among blind raters.

## Error analysis

Inspecting generations with high within-rubric dimension dispersion surfaces five recurring failure modes: correct outcomes reached via abstract argumentation rather than fact-bound *Subsumtion*; wrong-norm framing under otherwise plausible legal identification; perfunctory or missing *Subsumtion* in short answers; long but shallow generations that lexical metrics over-reward but the rubric does not; and correct fact-bound reasoning followed by a wrong final result. The last pattern is the cleanest judge-failure pointer — rubric-aligned scoring captures the reasoning/outcome dissociation that single-number metrics cannot. Per-pattern instance counts, representative task identifiers, dimension scores, and judge-justification quotes are reported in `Appendix~\ref{error-analysis-patterns}`{=latex}.

# Discussion

The five research questions together describe a consistent picture: closed-flagship LLMs lead the leaderboard across all three corpora (RQ1, RQ2) but the gap to the strongest open-weight systems narrows at the mid-tier; lexical surface metrics correlate with the rubric judge more strongly than embedding-based metrics (RQ3), and any single such metric would misrank systems at the flagship–mid-tier boundary relative to a rubric-aligned judge. The dimension-level cross-validation in @fig-rubric-dim is qualitatively consistent with Hufeld's inter-human pattern [@hufeld2024] — agreement is highest on dimensions with clearer scoring anchors and lowest on those depending more on layout and presentation judgement.

Human–AI co-creation (RQ4) outperforms unaided human work by `{python} fmtp(r4.get('mean_diff_ai_minus_trad'),0)` raw points, with the co-creation mean landing inside the closed-flagship-tier CI; non-independence and judge-style-alignment caveats are discussed in Limitations.

# Conclusion

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "the BenGER dataset" with "a benchmark";
drop ", all run end-to-end on our previously released BenGER platform
[@nagl2026benger]"; and replace the closing release sentence with
"The benchmark will be released as an open evaluation framework with
corpora, judge prompts, rubric, and analysis pipeline upon acceptance."
(no project name, no self-citation, no GitHub URL) -->
We introduce the BenGER dataset for subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law, combining an exam-style free-text corpus, a Benchathon subset with human and human–AI baselines, a rubric-aligned LLM-as-a-Judge, and a multi-rater cross-validation protocol — `{python} f"{len(systems_meta) if systems_meta else 12}"` systems and `{python} f"{sum((s.get('repetitions') or 0) for s in (systems_meta or [])) + (sum(r['n_generations'] for r in zjs_summary) if zjs_summary else 0) + (sum(r['n_generations'] for r in grundprinzipien_summary) if grundprinzipien_summary else 0)}"` generations on Benchathon, ZJS, and Doctrinal Principles, all run end-to-end on our previously released BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger]. Closed flagship systems lead, the strongest open-weight systems overlap with the closed mid-tier, and substituting the LLM judge for a human reviewer produces the same multi-rater pool agreement with the full-human reference as dropping that human altogether — supporting its use as a scalable rater alongside humans rather than as standalone ground truth. BenGER is released as an open evaluation framework with corpora, judge prompts, rubric, and analysis pipeline at <https://github.com/SebastianNagl/benger-platform>.

<!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: comment out the ENTIRE Future Work section
(the `# Future Work` heading plus all four `**…**` paragraphs that
follow). The blind submission caps body content at 8 pages and this
section identifies the present authors as the continuing maintainers
of the platform — both reasons to suppress it under ARR. Either
delete the section or wrap it in an HTML comment block. -->
# Future Work

The benchmark reported here is intentionally a first iteration: 12 systems, three corpora, one annotation round. We see four directions in which it should grow.

**Deep integration into German legal education.** German law students write *Probeklausuren* on a weekly cadence throughout their studies; today those exam answers are graded by overworked teaching assistants and then discarded. We plan to deploy the BenGER platform [@nagl2026benger] as the substrate for that weekly grading cycle at a growing set of German law faculties, with the LLM judge providing immediate first-pass formative feedback in the *Repetitor* style and human instructors providing a second-pass authoritative grade. The platform's role-aware design [@nagl2026benger] is built for exactly this two-tier loop, and the data flowing back from each round becomes the raw material for the next benchmark release.

**Broader judge pools and contamination probes.** The leaderboard reported here rests on a single primary judge (GPT-5-mini, cross-validated with Opus-4.7 and Gemini-3.1-Pro on the RQ5 subset). Future releases will re-score every generation with a multi-judge pool of at least three model families, and complement this with explicit contamination probes against the publicly indexed ZJS corpus.

**Task-specific rubrics for tighter scoring variance.** The *Falllösung* rubric used here scores the same ten dimensions across every exam-style case. We hypothesise that a rubric derived per task from its reference solution — extracting the specific norms, definitions, and *Subsumtion* steps the reference performs and scoring each one explicitly — would narrow the inter-rater variance reported in RQ5 by anchoring criteria to the case rather than to the case format. Prototyping such reference-derived rubrics on the Benchathon subset is a natural next step.

**Cross-jurisdiction and cross-task extension.** The *Obersatz–Definition–Subsumtion–Ergebnis* scaffold is shared directly with the other German-speaking civil-law jurisdictions (Austria, Switzerland), and through the 19th-century reception of German pandectist scholarship it also underpins legal education in many code-based systems further afield — Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Greece, and parts of Latin America (notably Brazil). The platform's metric and prompt-template layer is designed for incremental extension [@nagl2026benger], offering straightforward near-term paths to Austrian/Swiss exam corpora as well as to short-form *Hausarbeit* and oral-examination transcript scoring; the rubric structure itself should transfer with re-calibration to the broader pandectist-influenced jurisdictions.

# Limitations {.unnumbered}

Several limitations bound our findings.

**Black-box API evaluation.** All LLM systems are evaluated through hosted provider APIs in a black-box setting, so reported scores reflect end-to-end deployed behaviour, not isolated base-model capability. We do not vary provider-side prompts, decoding configurations, retrieval, or tool use, and so avoid claims about the contribution of individual architectural choices.

**Co-developed rubric and judge prompt.** The dimension rubric and the LLM-judge prompt were co-developed for this benchmark rather than adopted from independent prior work; we validate them as a pair against the multi-rater human-grading layer (RQ5), whose within-annotation inter-rater spread (`{python} fmt(r5_irr.get('mean_within_ann_spread_grade_points'),1)` grade points) sits within the range Hufeld [@hufeld2024] documents for German legal grading. Neither component has been validated in isolation.

**Modest Benchathon size.** The Benchathon subset comprises `{python} f"{n_benchathon}"` tasks and a modest participant pool. This suffices for the per-task, per-system comparisons and the judge validation reported here, but limits statistical power for fine-grained interaction effects such as expertise-stratified co-creation analyses.

**Judge-model bias.** The LLM judge is itself a model, and judge bias is a known confounder in evaluation [@zheng2023mtbench; @panickssery2024self]. We address this with two complementary checks: the multi-rater agreement protocol on the Benchathon validation subset (RQ5) and the inter-judge agreement analysis across three judge families (GPT-5-mini, Opus-4.7, Gemini-3.1-Pro) reported above, which shows a clear strictness gradient but pairwise rank agreement comparable in magnitude to inter-human agreement. <!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "in BenGER" with "in our benchmark". -->
Same-provider preference is a specific risk in BenGER because the primary judge GPT-5-mini (OpenAI) shares a model family with the leaderboard-leading systems GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.4-mini; we cannot fully rule out a same-family advantage of the kind documented in [@panickssery2024self]. Two bounds limit its plausible size: the cross-judge Spearman between GPT-5-mini and Opus-4.7 / Gemini-3.1-Pro (see RQ5) holds the system ordering across providers, and judge–human Pearson on the blind-reviewer subset is r=`{python} fmt(r5_jh.get('pearson_raw'),2)`. The headline LLM scores reported in RQ1–RQ2 nonetheless rest on a single judge model; cross-judge robustness of system rankings is descriptively supported by the inter-judge correlations on a subset, but the full RQ1–RQ2 leaderboard should be re-scored with at least one non-OpenAI judge (Opus-4.7 and Gemini-3.1-Pro are already in the RQ5 judge pool, but only GPT-5-mini scored every generation in RQ1–RQ2) before any strong cross-judge claim. The RQ5 reliability statistics themselves carry two method caveats: the Calderon-style alternative-annotator check is order-dependent for the single position chosen for the human leave-one-out, and the headline ICC is reported on the full k=`{python} f"{r5_irr.get('k_raters')}"` blind-rater pool over all `{python} f"{r5_irr.get('n_annotations')}"` picks.

**Jurisdictional scope.** The rubric is grounded in German doctrinal grading conventions and is not designed to generalise to common-law or non-German civil-law systems; <!-- BLIND-REVIEW SWAP: replace "in the BenGER dataset" with "in this dataset". -->
metrics and aggregate scores in the BenGER dataset should not be transferred to other jurisdictions without re-calibration.

**Pretraining contamination on ZJS.** ZJS material is publicly indexed, so provider models may have encountered some of it during pretraining; we have not run an explicit memorisation probe. The Benchathon and Doctrinal Principles corpora, which remain private until publication, are not a clean contamination control: their orderings agreeing with the ZJS leaderboard is consistent with both low contamination and with contamination that scales proportionally across systems. What the agreement does bound is *catastrophic* differential contamination — a system whose ZJS rank is driven by memorisation and that would underperform on the unseen Benchathon and Doctrinal Principles items — since such a system would visibly fall out of order on the private corpora. Per-domain CIs (RQ4), the pairwise inter-judge correlations (RQ5), and the patterns reported in §Error analysis are presented descriptively without multiple-comparison correction; we do not draw inferential claims from individual cell-level comparisons.

**Rubric divergence on Doctrinal Principles.** The Benchathon and ZJS exam-style corpora are graded with the 10-dimension *Falllösung* rubric, while the short-form Doctrinal Principles corpus uses a 4-dimension rubric (result correctness, legal knowledge, subsumption, clarity); per-dimension comparisons across corpora are therefore not meaningful, and only aggregate raw scores, pass rates, and Ja/Nein accuracy (Doctrinal Principles only) should be compared across the three.

**Google-Flash model drift.** The Google efficiency-tier slot was generated with two distinct Google models — Gemini-3-Flash on the Benchathon subset and Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite (a separate efficiency product line rather than a successor release of Flash) on the ZJS and Doctrinal Principles corpora — because the model set was finalised between the two generation runs. We treat them as the same conceptual tier slot for tier-level comparisons and disclose the raw model ids in `Table~\ref{tbl-system-overview}`{=latex}; row-level cross-corpus comparison for this slot should be read with the model drift in mind, and tier-level claims should not be read as a per-model claim about either Flash or Flash-Lite alone.

# Ethical Considerations {.unnumbered}
